question: What is FC?
ground_truth: Fully Connected layers
context:
Convolutional Neural Networks CNN
Why fully connected?
Each neuron connects to every element in the flattened
vector.
Allows complex, non-linear combinations of high-level
features.
Softmax: Converts scores to probabilities that sum to 1.
Flattening converts learned spatial 
feature maps into a vector, then FC-
layers combine these features non-
linearly, ending with softmax to turn 
raw class scores into probabilities, 
CNN learns the entire pipeline from 
pixels to probabilities end-to-end.

question: What is a spatial filter?
ground_truth: A window with some width and height that is usually much less than that of the image.
context:
CNN Convolution Concept
Convolution: It is the process of applying a filter (kernel) to an image to extract features or
modify the image (blurring, sharpening, edge detection, etc.).
Do we Need Padding?
Convolution = sliding a kernel over an image, multiplying and summing at each
position.
The spatial filter is a window with some width and height that is usually much less than
that of the image. Mostly 3×3, 5×5 or 7×7 size filters are used. The values in the filter are
called coefficients or weights. There are other terms to call filters such as mask, kernel,
template, or window. An example of spatial filter is shown above.

question: What is an image gradient?
ground_truth: A directional change in the intensity in an image
context:
An image gradient is a directional change in the intensity in
an image. The image gradient is one of the fundamental
building blocks in image processing.
Image gradients have been used as a measure of image
sharpness. The gradient at any pixel is the derivative of the
values of neighbouring pixels.
Generally sharper images have higher gradient values. Thus,
any
image
fusion
method
should
result
in
increased
gradient values because this process makes the images
sharper compared to the low resolution image.
Sharpening is opposite to the blurring. In blurring, we reduce the edge
content and in Sharpening, we increase the edge content.
Intermediate Gradient

question: What is the Generator?
ground_truth: A model that creates fake data
context:
Teacher (Loss Function & Gradient Descent)
Concept:
How does the Generator learn to make good fakes? 
It needs feedback. 
We use our original CNN (Feature Extractor) again!
The Twist:
We retrain this CNN, but we change its job. Instead of 
identifying "Cat" vs. "Dog," 
we train it to identify "Real" vs. "Fake.“
Result:
This new CNN is the Discriminator. 
It gives the Generator a "Loss Score" (how fake it looks).
Analogy

question: What is CGAN?
ground_truth: Conditional GAN
context:
Types of GAN
2. Conditional GAN (CGAN) 
Conditional GAN (CGAN) adds an additional conditional parameter to guide the
generation process. Instead of generating data randomly they allow the model to
produce specific types of outputs. Working of CGANs:
A conditional variable (y) is fed into both the generator and the discriminator.
This ensures that the generator creates data corresponding to the given condition
(e.g. generating images of specific objects).
The discriminator also receives the labels to help distinguish between real and
fake data.
Example: Instead of generating any random image, CGAN can generate a specific
object like a dog or a cat based on the label.
3. Deep Convolutional GAN (DCGAN)
Deep
Convolutional
GAN
(DCGAN)
are
among the most popular types of GANs
used for image generation.
4. Laplacian Pyramid GAN (LAPGAN)
Laplacian Pyramid GAN (LAPGAN), designed
to generate ultra-high-quality images by
using a multi-resolution approach.

question: What is a neural network?
ground_truth: A machine that will sort the fruit according to type based on sensor outputs
context:
Artificial Neural Networks ANN
When fruit is brought to the warehouse, various types of fruit may be mixed together.
The dealer wants a machine that will sort the fruit according to type. There is a
conveyer belt on which the fruit is loaded. This conveyer passes through a set of
sensors, which measure three properties of the fruit: shape, texture and weight.
The three sensor outputs will then be input to a neural network to decide the right
place. let’s assume that there are only two kinds of fruit on the conveyor: apples and
oranges.

question: What is Median filtering?
ground_truth: Assigns the mid value of all the gray levels in the mask to the center of mask
context:

Nonlinear spatial filters, the output is based on order of gray levels in the
masked area (sub-image). Examples: Median filtering, Max & Min
filtering
Median filtering

Assigns the mid value of all the gray levels in the mask to the center of
mask;
Particularly effective when:
– The noise pattern consists of strong, spiky components (impulse
noise, salt-and-pepper)
– Edges are to be preserved, it force points with distinct gray levels to
be more like their neighbors
Order Statistics Filter: It is based on the ordering the pixels contained in
the image area encompassed by the filter. It replaces the value of the
center pixel with the value determined by the ranking result.
Order Statistics Filters
Extreme outliers (like random "salt-and-pepper noise") are completely ignored because
the median is robust to outliers.

question: What is CCD?
ground_truth: Charge-Coupled Device
context:
Use CCD for Scientific AI (Medical diagnosis) where image
quality > speed.
Use CMOS for Edge AI (Smartphones, Drones, Security
cameras) where speed (FPS) & power efficiency > perfect
quality.
Example: A Tesla uses CMOS (high speed, lower quality)
because driving at 100mph requires high Frame Rate, not
perfect ISO noise levels.
AI Engineering Trade-Off

question: What is KNN?
ground_truth: K-Nearest Neighbors
context:
KNN Pros & Cons
12
KNN Pros:
Simple to understand and implement.
No training phase: it just stores the data. Makes it fast to "train" on small datasets.
Naturally handles multi-class problems.
KNN Cons:
Can be slow with big datasets (must calculate distance to every training point for 
each prediction).
Sensitive to irrelevant features (all features are treated equally in the distance
calculation).
Performance depends heavily on the choice of K and the distance metric.

question: What is Super AI?
ground_truth: Artificial super-intelligence (ASI) that exceeds the human intellectual ability
context:
AI: Classification 
AI Classification 3- Super AI
Super AI is AI that exceeds the human intellectual ability. It’s also
known as artificial super-intelligence (ASI).
It’s the best at everything. Even the brightest human minds cannot come close to
the abilities of super AI.
University of Oxford super-intelligence is "any intellect that greatly exceeds the
cognitive performance of humans in virtually all domains of interest".
Such a level of AI is still hypothetical and remains a subject of ongoing research
and development.

question: What is Supervised Learning?
ground_truth: A type of machine learning where the model is trained on labeled data to predict the output on the basis of prior experiences.
context:
Pros and Cons of Supervised Learning
Advantages
• The model can predict the output on the basis of prior experiences.
• We can have an exact idea about the classes of objects.
• Supervised learning model helps us to solve various real-world problems
such as fraud detection, spam filtering
Disadvantages
• Models are not suitable for handling the complex tasks.
• Cannot predict the correct output if the test data is different from the
training dataset.
• Training required lots of computation times.
• We need enough knowledge about the classes of object.
44

question: What is the purpose of splitting the training dataset?
ground_truth: Into training dataset, test dataset, and validation dataset
context:
1.
First Determine problem domain and the type of training dataset (text or multimedia)
2.
Collect/Gather the labelled training data.
3.
Split the training dataset into training dataset, test dataset, and validation dataset.
4.
Determine the input features of the training dataset, which should have enough
knowledge so that the model can accurately predict the output.
5.
Determine the suitable algorithm for the model, such as support vector machine,
decision tree, etc.
6.
Execute the algorithm on the training dataset. Sometimes we need validation sets as
the control parameters, which are the subset of training datasets.
7.
Evaluate the accuracy of the model by providing the test set. If the model predicts
the correct output, which means our model is accurate.
42

6.
Execute the algorithm on the training dataset. Sometimes we need validation sets as
the control parameters, which are the subset of training datasets.
7.
Evaluate the accuracy of the model by providing the test set. If the model predicts
the correct output, which means our model is accurate.
42
Steps Involved in Supervised Learning:

question: What is Veracity?
ground_truth: The trustworthiness and quality of the data
context:
•
Volume
Volume: Big data involves vast amounts of data generated from various
sources, including sensors, social media, transaction records, etc....
•
Velocity
Velocity: Big data is generated at high speeds and requires real-time or near-
real-time processing to extract insights and value from it.
•
Variety
Variety: Big data encompasses diverse types of data, including text, images,
videos, sensor data, social media posts, and more, making it heterogeneous and
complex to analyze.
•
Veracity
Veracity: It refers to the trustworthiness and quality of the data. With such a
high volume of data generated daily, it remains a challenge to ensure the data
you work with is unbiased and correctly represents what it’s supposed to.
•
Value
Value: Data on its own isn’t useful unless it is analyzed and transformed into
meaningful
insights
that
provide
value
to
the
business.
For
example,
businesses use customer purchase data to create personalized marketing
strategies or predict future buying behavior.
32
Characteristics

question: What is a rational agent?
ground_truth: An agent that maximizes the expected value of their goal given what they currently know
context:
AI: Thinking vs. Acting Humanly
Acting Rationally?
The study of rational agents agents that maximize the expected value of
their goal given what they currently know.
Rational agents acts in a way to maximize their goals.
Agent is anything that senses its environment and acts (able to execute actions) which
can be
1- Human,
2- Robot, or
3- Pure software.
- Sensors

eye
/
camera
/ Listener to byte stream
- Actuators 
hand
/
motors
/ ports-sockets
AI is concerned with the production of intelligent agents (Agentic AI). So, do
all agents are intelligent?
Intelligent Agents are agents that can operate autonomously in complex
environments.
